Okay, welcome back to the three metastates of an empowered sale. We are going to start with what I believe to be the most important, the most vital, because if you don't have this, you definitely don't have someone connected to their power and you definitely don't have someone connected to their vision. So we're going to start off by helping our prospect get and remain connected to their sense of safety. So here's why this is vital to do it, because if you don't do it, if you don't create safety on your sales calls, your prospect will automatically, unconsciously, and what I would deem to be quite appropriately, set up their own defenses, armorings, and distancing. So what are they protecting against? If you don't establish safety and they are setting up defenses, what are they going to typically set up those defenses from? Well, there's a lot that is happening in a sales call. They might be protecting against the shame and making a poor decision that they'll regret. The embarrassment of getting screwed over, right? Many people you talk to may still be carrying this wound of being screwed over and victimized by the last opportunity that they had or some other traumatic experience in their business. Or maybe it's the fear of being seen as small or inferior in relation to you, someone who allowed themselves to get screwed over and now has to wear that shame and embarrassment that counters their identity as someone who's in control and makes wise decisions. So a lot of dissonance there. It could be the fear of being overpowered and the smallness in succumbing to these unbalanced power dynamics. It could be the fear of an uncertain future. So something that has nothing to do with you, but just the bigness of the opportunity at hand and the uncertainty of that even happening, right? They could have a fear that even if they make this investment, the outcome won't happen because of self-doubt, because of limiting beliefs, because of an unrecognized past, or even simply because no future is guaranteed no matter what they do, right? So that might be a fear they're holding onto and defending against. It could be the fear that you won't have their best interests in hand and will force them into a decision they don't want to make or force compliance as we spoke about in the last module, or the fear that they know won't be respected and you'll shame, pressure, and ultimately twist it into a yes. So there is a lot that they could be potentially protecting against. Obviously, this isn't an exhaustive list. Someone isn't going to be going through exactly these seven things, but this is a good 90-10 of what folks will typically be protecting from defending against on these calls unless you establish and maintain safety all throughout. So creating safety doesn't make your calls these sterile and surfacing experiences, right? Paradoxically, it's what makes it safe to go deep, to get vulnerable and unpack as much as is necessary to move through those milestones we spoke about and make an empowered decision at the end of the call. So there are several types of safety we need to establish. If we're going to break this down a little bit more. So the first one, the big one is safety and working with you, right? They need to feel the genuine care and trust that you won't let them make a poor decision and that you're actually looking up for them. So phrases that could reinforce or establish this throughout, right? Very simple. I won't let you make a poor decision today. My only interest is in helping you get clarity on your most effective next steps. We'll only explore an invitation if we both completely agree that it may be a fit at this time. I'm simply stating setting the intention. I want this to be stress-free. I really don't believe that this should be scary in any way, shape or form. I want you to come into this program feeling confident and excited. What would make that possible for you? So these are phrases. Don't necessarily use them verbatim, but keep them in mind and maybe you can use them as verbatim if it works its way into your call. But these are phrases that can establish a sense of safety in working with you. So one thing I used to do at the beginning of every call, right? And it was also to kind of make sure that we were both in a good place, so we both essentially had zero attachment, right? To the yes or the no that they felt safe with either direction, right? But ultimately, I wanted someone to be able to own their answer by the end of the sales call if an invitation was present, right? So I call this the yes, no, decharger, right? So have them get in touch with what a yes feels in their body, what a no feels in their body and feel safety expressing either, right? And then I literally had people go through saying yes, no, yes, no, yes, no, yes, no, yes, no, yes, no, yes, no, yes, no, yes, no, yes, no, until they felt comfortable expressing either to me. And what this did as a result was when that invitation was made, right? Someone knew what a yes would feel like and they had the power to say it and someone knew what a no would feel like, right? And they felt totally comfortable and at ease to say no, because both of them had been attuned to early in our call and both of them had been practiced, right? With me present, receiving both that yes and that no. So this isn't necessarily something that's going to be appropriate for every business, for every context, every situation, but at least it should point to the level of comfort someone needs to have in a yes and a no with you, right? And their comfort and safety in actually feeling what their true answer is, right? And their comfort level in expressing it. So this is probably the most powerful way to create and amplify safety in you. It's to point out potential concerns before they do. This is something that you're typically going to be doing in the middle of the call or more towards the end, but draw attention to where you may see a chance of them not being able to succeed and ask them what they think about it, give them a chance to respond. So as long as you are pointing out the potential pitfalls before they do, before they even have a chance to think of it, you will earn that trust and resonance of them feeling like you're protecting them from a potentially poor decision. And you'll also invite power and resourcefulness in them thinking of the solution. Since the problem was already brought up by you, they feel safe to actually be in their power, right? That you are watching out for them, you are pointing out the potential pitfalls and they can remain more solution focused than trying to scan every inch and crevice of the opportunity for any potential pitfalls that you may not have been aware of or may not have been willing to point out for them. So what could this look like? It could look like thanks so much for opening up about that, Jane. I'll be honest, I still believe our AI program can be a great fit for you and help you set up an epic foundation. But given that you're in early phases of building your list, I do want to draw attention to the fact that it might take a bit longer than others for you to recoup your ROI. Are you prepared for that? Right? So I'm drawing attention to a potential pitfall and giving Jane in this case the opportunity to respond in kind, to get resourceful, to talk about what it might look like to prepare for a longer path towards ROI, right? Or to discuss other more short term opportunities for ROI as a foundation is built. Another one, perhaps in a relationship coaching context. Erica, I totally understand the challenges you've had in getting your partner to fully commit to doing deep work together. So we do provide specific coaching to help partners get over the shame, fear and resistance in the in engaging in couples work. And I'm confident we'll get there. But before we explore an invitation, I just want to make sure you'd be comfortable if in the short term it took a little bit of work to get there, right? So we are drawing attention to a potential challenge pitfall or lack of fit between the client and our program before they have a chance to think about it and wonder and question. Why wouldn't they have warned me about that? Right? Why is it so obvious to me that there's this potential misfit and they didn't draw attention to it first, right? That's what creates a lack of trust and a lack of safety. Next, they need to feel safety in the outcome. So most people you speak to will have a fear of failure and need safety around their own potential for future disappointment. So throughout the conversation, create reassurance and confidence and even certainty where you can ethically do so in the outcome by talking about it casually, right? Normalizing the results referring to other clients with similar starting points and using confident language around the future outcomes that you believe they will be able to achieve through your help. So sales is alchemical. It's shifting one state from a familiar past into a less certain feature. And it's your job to bring as much safety and certainty into that new outcome, which will inevitably always feel scarier to begin with, especially if it's something that someone hasn't achieved before, which is why they're on the call. So a few phrases that could go a long way. I really see you succeeding with this. Given how committed you've been to growing your freelance career and how much you've already achieved in such a short amount of time, I have no doubt that you'll fully replace your corporate income within four months. I believe you'll find that within three weeks, you'll be amazed by how fast your relationship can improve. By week three, your energy levels will make you feel like a totally new person. By the second month of our work together, you won't even recognize yourself as you boldly ask for bigger project fees and pursue opportunities you thought were reserved for others. I promise you, it's going to feel amazing. So this is where they need to feel your confidence, sense of security and sense of certainty and the results available to them that they don't yet quite believe, right? Because they haven't actually achieved it yet. And they're almost afraid to claim that sense of possibility with so much fear of failure still wrapped into it. Next, you can create safety by adjusting your pace. So if they're more chill, more laid back, more introverted, and you're talking so fast that they can't even really process everything you're saying, you'll inherently feel threatening, right? Especially if your talking speed is coming off as an expression of your own fear and insecurity. So slow down. That's really the invitation. You don't need to match tonality and demeanor, right? Classic NLP style. But in most cases, slowing down your pace by 20%, 30% will create a greater sense of ease and relaxation on the nervous system, which will help someone feel more safe in that container. So we're going to talk a little bit more about pacing in the next module. But the quick note there is just intend to slow down your pace just a little bit more than you probably already are. Cool. So that's what we got. That is how we are establishing and reinforcing safety both on the beginning of the call and throughout the call. And this is really the foundation, which is going to allow someone to access their power and stay connected to this sense of possibility. It all starts with safety. So take notes on this and tend to establish it on your next call. And I'll meet you in the next lesson where we're going to talk about connecting one to their own sense of power. So I'll see you there.
